records.commonRootPattern: Two roots are most common in the course slide (61%).
records.importantVariation: One root is reported in 38%, while three roots are reported in 1%.
records.clinicalNote: Count roots and canals separately; the external root form does not make the canal count automatic.
wrongOptions: Three roots are the common pattern: palatal, mesiobuccal, and distobuccal.
Two roots are common, but they are mesial and distal roots.
One root is the only course-described pattern.
records.explanation: Source conflict held for faculty review: slide 15 reports two roots at 61%, while slide 22 says two roots at 90%. This item does not enter scored play until that conflict is adjudicated.

sourceRefs.locator: Slides 15 and 22 (conflicting root-prevalence text; held from Challenge)

records.commonRootPattern: Three roots are the common pattern: palatal, mesiobuccal, and distobuccal.
records.commonCanalPattern: Four canals are the course teaching pattern across those three roots.
records.importantVariation: The mesiobuccal root may contain a second canal (MB2).
records.clinicalNote: A three-root tooth can still have four canals because the mesiobuccal root may contain two.
wrongOptions: Two roots are arranged as a broad buccal root and a palatal root.
Two roots are common, arranged as mesial and distal roots.
One root is the only course-described pattern.
records.explanation: The maxillary first molar commonly has three named roots. Root count and canal count diverge when the mesiobuccal root contains a second canal.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy and Occlusion Textbook Companion.pdf

records.commonRootPattern: Two roots are common, arranged as mesial and distal roots.
records.commonCanalPattern: Three canals are common: two in the mesial root and one in the distal root.
records.importantVariation: Four canals are listed at 35%, and the distal root can contain two canals.
records.clinicalNote: The broad mesial root carries two-canal logic; do not assume the distal root must be single-canal.
wrongOptions: Three roots are the common pattern: palatal, mesiobuccal, and distobuccal.
Three roots are arranged as two mesial roots and one distal root.
One root is the only course-described pattern.
records.explanation: The mandibular first molar commonly has two roots, one mesial and one distal. The course then separately teaches a common three-canal pattern.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy and Occlusion Textbook Companion.pdf

records.commonRootPattern: Two roots are common: mesial and distal, usually closer and more parallel than on the first molar.
records.commonCanalPattern: Three canals are the course teaching pattern: two mesial and one distal.
records.importantVariation: Four canals are listed at 8% in the course deck.
records.clinicalNote: The closer root form does not eliminate canal variation; root and canal counts remain separate observations.
wrongOptions: Three roots are the common pattern: palatal, mesiobuccal, and distobuccal.
Three roots are arranged as two mesial roots and one distal root.
One root is the only course-described pattern.
records.explanation: The mandibular second molar commonly has mesial and distal roots that are closer together and more parallel than those of the first molar.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy and Occlusion Textbook Companion.pdf

records.commonRootPattern: Two roots are most common in the course slide (61%).
records.importantVariation: One root is reported in 38%, while three roots are reported in 1%.
records.clinicalNote: Canal prevalence is not the same statistic as root prevalence.
wrongOptions: Four canals are the course teaching pattern across three roots.
Three canals are common: two in the mesial root and one in the distal root.
One canal is the only course-described pattern.
records.explanation: Source conflict held for faculty review: slide 15 reports two canals at 90% and two roots at 61%, while slide 22 says two roots at 90%. This item does not enter scored play until that conflict is adjudicated.

sourceRefs.locator: Slides 15 and 22 (conflicting root-prevalence text; held from Challenge)

records.commonRootPattern: Three roots are the common pattern: palatal, mesiobuccal, and distobuccal.
records.commonCanalPattern: Four canals are the course teaching pattern across those three roots.
records.importantVariation: The extra canal is usually sought in the mesiobuccal root as MB2.
records.clinicalNote: Think three roots, four canals: one palatal, one distobuccal, and potentially two mesiobuccal canals.
wrongOptions: Two canals are reported in 90%.
Three canals are common: two in the mesial root and one in the distal root.
Four separate roots each contain one canal.
records.explanation: The course cross-section slide teaches three roots and four canals for the maxillary first molar. The fourth canal reflects canal duplication within a root, not a fourth root.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy and Occlusion Textbook Companion.pdf

records.commonRootPattern: Two roots are common, arranged as mesial and distal roots.
records.commonCanalPattern: Three canals are common: two in the mesial root and one in the distal root.
records.importantVariation: Four canals are listed at 35%, and the distal root can contain two canals.
records.clinicalNote: The mesial root commonly contains two canals; the distal canal count can vary.
wrongOptions: Four canals are the course teaching pattern across three roots.
Two canals are reported in 90%.
Three canals are divided one per root across three roots.
records.explanation: For the common three-canal pattern, two canals are in the broad mesial root and one is in the distal root. That is three canals across two roots.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy and Occlusion Textbook Companion.pdf

records.commonRootPattern: Two roots are common: mesial and distal, usually closer and more parallel than on the first molar.
records.commonCanalPattern: Three canals are the course teaching pattern: two mesial and one distal.
records.importantVariation: Four canals are listed at 8% in the course deck.
records.clinicalNote: Use the three-canal pattern as a common model, while preserving the course-described four-canal variation.
wrongOptions: Four canals are the course teaching pattern across three roots.
Two canals are reported in 90%.
Three canals are divided one per root across three roots.
records.explanation: The course review describes two roots and three canals for mandibular molars, then gives a separate four-canal prevalence for the second molar.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy and Occlusion Textbook Companion.pdf

records.commonRootPattern: Two roots are most common in the course slide (61%).
records.importantVariation: One root is reported in 38%, while three roots are reported in 1%.
records.clinicalNote: Use the prevalence distribution to avoid turning a common pattern into an absolute rule.
wrongOptions: A second mesiobuccal canal may be present within a three-root molar plan.
A mandibular first molar can show an additional distal canal.
A four-canal mandibular second-molar pattern is listed at 8%.
records.explanation: Source conflict held for faculty review: slide 15 distributes the roots as 61% two, 38% one, and 1% three, while slide 22 says two roots at 90%. This item does not enter scored play until adjudicated.

sourceRefs.locator: Slides 15 and 22 (conflicting root-prevalence text; held from Challenge)

records.commonRootPattern: Three roots are the common pattern: palatal, mesiobuccal, and distobuccal.
records.commonCanalPattern: Four canals are the course teaching pattern across those three roots.
records.importantVariation: The mesiobuccal root may contain MB2; a cited clinical sample found 70.2% with four or more canals, and about 99% of fourth canals were in the mesiobuccal root.
records.clinicalNote: Carefully inspect the pulpal floor for a second mesiobuccal canal instead of equating three roots with three canals.
wrongOptions: A mandibular first molar can show an additional distal canal.
Four canals are listed at 35%, and the distal root can contain two canals.
Four canals are listed at 8% in the course deck.
records.explanation: The deck's cited JADA sample describes its own clinical population: 70.2% had four or more canals, and about 99% of fourth canals were in the mesiobuccal root. It supports an MB2 search clue, not a guarantee for every tooth.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy and Occlusion Textbook Companion.pdf

records.commonRootPattern: Two roots are common, arranged as mesial and distal roots.
records.commonCanalPattern: Three canals are common: two in the mesial root and one in the distal root.
records.importantVariation: Four canals are listed at 35%, and the distal root can contain two canals.
records.clinicalNote: The common one-distal-canal model is a starting point; distal-root variation remains important.
wrongOptions: The mesiobuccal root may contain MB2, with the fourth canal usually located there.
The roots of a mandibular second molar are usually closer and more parallel than those of the first molar.
Four canals are listed at 8% in the course deck.
records.explanation: The deck lists a four-canal pattern at 35% for the mandibular first molar and separately notes that the distal root can have two canals. Keep the three-canal pattern common, not absolute.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy and Occlusion Textbook Companion.pdf

records.commonRootPattern: Two roots are common: mesial and distal, usually closer and more parallel than on the first molar.
records.commonCanalPattern: Three canals are the course teaching pattern: two mesial and one distal.
records.importantVariation: Four canals are listed at 8% in the course deck.
records.clinicalNote: The 8% figure marks a meaningful variation without displacing the common three-canal model.
wrongOptions: The mesiobuccal root may contain MB2, with the fourth canal usually located there.
Three named roots are common for the maxillary first molar.
Four canals are listed at 35%, and the distal root can contain two canals.
records.explanation: The mandibular molar review slide lists four canals at 8% for the second molar. Present it as a course-described variation, not as the default anatomy.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy and Occlusion Textbook Companion.pdf
